items.q: What did Pollock mean by his drip paintings being 'in the painting'?
items.correct: He worked from all sides, making the act of painting central to the meaning.
items.misconception: He literally fell into wet paint.
items.why: Students take 'in the painting' literally. Pollock meant his BODILY ENGAGEMENT - working around and over the canvas on the floor - was integral to the work, not just the marks left behind.

items.q: What is the difference between relief and intaglio printing?
items.correct: Relief prints from raised surfaces; intaglio prints from incised lines below the surface.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Relief is for paper, intaglio is for fabric.
items.why: Students don't grasp the inverse logic. Lino cuts AWAY non-printing areas (relief - raised inked); etching incises lines that HOLD ink below the surface (intaglio - ink pressed up into paper).

items.q: What did Renaissance artists rediscover that medieval art lacked?
items.correct: Linear perspective and naturalistic representation of the human body.
items.why: Students assume medieval art was colourless. It was vivid but flat - the Renaissance breakthrough was mathematical PERSPECTIVE (Brunelleschi) and anatomical realism, not the introduction of colour.

items.q: How many resolved artworks does an IBDP HL exhibition require minimum?
items.why: Correct answer: 8. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1976 essay critiques the white cube gallery?
items.why: It's O'Doherty. “Krauss” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Documenta first open in Kassel?
items.why: The real figure is 1955 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
